g.data-narration: Welcome! This lecture covers Virtual Functions in C++, a powerful feature enabling runtime polymorphism.
A Virtual Function is a member function in a base class that can be overridden in derived classes, enabling dynamic function resolution at runtime.
g.text: A function declared with 'virtual' keyword in base class that derived classes can override

g.data-narration: Runtime Polymorphism allows the program to call the correct function based on the actual object type, not the pointer type used.
g.text: Same code produces different results based on actual object type:

g.data-narration: The virtual keyword enables dynamic binding. The override keyword (C++11) ensures the function signature matches the base class.
Every class with virtual functions has a vtable - a static array of function pointers. Each object has a vptr pointing to its class vtable.
When calling a virtual function, the compiler follows the vptr to find the vtable, then calls the function at the appropriate offset.
A Pure Virtual Function is declared with '= 0' and has no implementation. It forces derived classes to provide their own implementation.
An Abstract Class contains at least one pure virtual function. It cannot be instantiated but can have pointers/references for polymorphism.
g.text: Cannot: Shape s; ✓ Can: Shape* s;

g.data-narration: Virtual destructors ensure proper cleanup when deleting derived objects through base class pointers. Always use virtual destructors in polymorphic classes.
g.text: Rule: If class has virtual functions, destructor MUST be virtual
virtual ~Base() { } // Always virtual in polymorphic classes

g.data-narration: Without virtual destructor: only base destructor called, causing memory leaks. With virtual: derived destructor runs first, then base destructor.
Object Slicing occurs when a derived object is assigned to a base object by value. The derived part is 'sliced off', losing polymorphism.
g.text: Derived d; Base b = d; // Slicing! b is a Base, not Derived
Use pointers or references: Base* b = &d; or Base& b = d;

g.data-narration: The override keyword (C++11) explicitly marks a function as overriding a virtual function. Compiler catches signature mismatches.
g.text: Without override: silently creates NEW virtual function if signature differs

g.data-narration: Never call virtual functions from constructors! During base construction, the derived part doesn't exist yet, so virtual calls resolve to base version.
g.text: 12. Don't Call Virtual Functions in Constructors!
Base() { display(); } // Calls Base::display, NOT Derived!
Derived part doesn't exist yet during base constructor execution

g.data-narration: Here's a practical example: different account types (BankAccount, SavingsAccount, CurrentAccount) calculate interest differently using virtual functions.
Abstract classes define interfaces. Here Shape is abstract with pure virtual area(). Circle and Rectangle implement it, enabling polymorphic area calculation.
Key takeaways: Use virtual for runtime polymorphism, =0 for pure virtual, always virtual destructor, avoid slicing, use override keyword, don't call virtuals in constructors.
